original_abstract: Neural machine translation (NMT) has recently emerged as a compelling alternative to traditional statistical machine translation by utilizing deep neural networks to map source sequences to target sequences. Most NMT architectures employ an encoder-decoder framework, where an encoder compresses a variable-length input sentence into a fixed-size vector representation from which a decoder generates the translation. In this study, we investigate the empirical properties of NMT systems by comparing the established RNN Encoder-Decoder with a novel architecture featuring a gated recursive convolutional neural network (grConv) encoder. Through a series of experiments on English-to-French translation, we demonstrate that while these models excel at translating short sentences with familiar vocabularies, their performance declines sharply as sentence length increases or when they encounter out-of-vocabulary words. Additionally, our qualitative analysis reveals that the proposed grConv model is capable of capturing internal linguistic hierarchies, automatically learning a form of syntactic structure without explicit grammatical supervision. Our findings highlight current limitations in the representational capacity of fixed-length embeddings and suggest critical directions for improving the robustness of neural translation models.
humanized_abstract: Recent advancements in deep learning have resulted in neural machine translation (NMT) gaining the attentions as an effective substitute for the well known statistical machine translation (SMT) method. Many works in the field have shown that neural networks with appropriate settings can outperform SMT systems, thus representing a novel approach for machine translation, which was originally accomplished using language specific knowledge encoded into statistical translation algorithms. Most NMT approaches use the widely recognized encoder–decoder paradigm, in which the encoder embeds the input sentence as a fixed-length vector that the decoder then uses to predict the output word. We examine empirically the characteristics of modern translation algorithms and the effects that different approaches and modifications can introduce into the overall translation accuracy. Specifically, we take the basic form of the RNN Encoder–Decoder architecture as our baseline, and we develop and assess a more complex version of the encoding component by proposing gated recursive convolutional (grConv) neural network models to improve the translation accuracy achieved by their classical counter parts. Experimental evaluation is carried out on well known English-to-French benchmarks (WMT13–16), where it is shown that, similarly to previous studies, both approaches tend to exhibit great aptitude for short inputs consisting of brief and vocabularies that are well known from the training datasets; yet they significantly degrade their performance, on those benchmarks, as the length of input sentences is increasing and when the input sentence contains words not seen before during training. Further, qualitative examples indicate that, while trained only on fluent input texts (i.e. the training datasets) the developed grConv translation component is able to obtain meaningful representations for some words in their corresponding translated outputs that signify an inner structure depicting a type of syntactic structure in the generated translation output, that is built to be naturally understood to human readers without requiring any additional, knowledge specific, part-of-speech tagging provided as additional supervisory signals to the basic training procedure. Our empirical results further demonstrate that there is great need for further developments that address the representation capacity of the fixed vector embedding space that forms the basic concept underlying today’s basic form of neural translation models.
